response: Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-abc", "next_review_date": "2026-04-15"}]. The next_review_date parameter directly sets when the project comes up for review, overriding the interval-based calculation.
Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-xyz", "completed_by_children": true}]. This enables auto-completion when the last remaining task is checked off.

Call get_tasks with task_id set to the task's ID (or query='Weekly team standup'). The returned fields include nextDueDate, which shows the next occurrence date for recurring tasks without needing to complete the current one.
Call get_projects with stalled_only=true. This returns active projects with no available next actions — exactly the stuck/stalled projects that need attention or new tasks.

No tool call needed for the explanation. The catchUpAutomatically field on the task determines the behavior. Call get_tasks with the task's ID to check the catchUpAutomatically field. If true, completing it produces one catch-up occurrence (not 5 separate ones). If false, each missed interval spawns its own occurrence. The repetitionMethod also matters: 'due_after_completion' bases the next due date on when you complete it, so missed days don't pile up regardless.

No tool call needed — this is an explanation scenario. If the 'Priority' parent tag has childrenAreMutuallyExclusive set to true, then assigning 'Low' via add_tags will silently remove 'High' automatically. Check get_tags to see if the Priority parent tag has childrenAreMutuallyExclusive: true. If it does, the add_tags call will replace 'High' with 'Low'. If not, both tags will coexist. To verify, call get_tags and look at the Priority parent tag's childrenAreMutuallyExclusive field.

Call create_projects with [{"name": "Q3 Report", "folder_path": "Work", "due_date": "2026-07-15", "defer_date": "2026-06-01"}]. The defer_date hides the project until June 1, and due_date sets the July 15 deadline.
Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-abc", "due_date": ""}]. Passing an empty string clears the due date.
Call get_projects with project_id='proj-xyz'. The response includes dueDate, deferDate, and plannedDate fields.

Call update_tasks with [{"id": "task-101", "parent_task_id": "task-200"}]. This moves the task from the inbox to become a subtask of 'Draft report'. Note: project_id and parent_task_id are mutually exclusive on create, but update_tasks accepts parent_task_id to reparent a task.
Call get_tasks with query='mortgage'. This searches task names and notes case-insensitively across all projects.
Call get_tags (no parameters). The response includes parentTagId for each tag. Find the tag named 'High' and check its parentTagId — if it matches the ID of the 'Energy' tag, it's nested under it.
Call update_tasks with [{"id": "task-500", "status": "dropped"}]. This drops/abandons the task without deleting it. Note: this task is not recurring, so no need to clear recurrence first.

Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-A", "status": "dropped"}, {"id": "proj-B", "status": "dropped"}, {"id": "proj-C", "status": "dropped"}]. This marks all three as dropped/abandoned without deleting them.

No tool call needed — this is an explanation. Tasks in a completed project retain their own completed=false status. The task's completed field reflects only the task's own state, not its container's. However, those tasks will have available=false because the project is done. Use get_tasks with available_only=true to filter them out. The tasks are showing up because the default query doesn't exclude tasks in completed projects — it only checks the task's own completed field.
Call reorder_project with project_id='proj-C', before_project_id='proj-A'. This places proj-C before proj-A, making it first in the folder.

Three calls in sequence:
1. create_tags with [{"name": "Priority", "children_are_mutually_exclusive": true}] — creates the parent tag with mutual exclusivity enabled.
2. create_tags with [{"name": "High", "parent_tag": "Priority"}, {"name": "Medium", "parent_tag": "Priority"}, {"name": "Low", "parent_tag": "Priority"}] — creates the three child tags under Priority.

Call update_tags with [{"id": "tag-energy-001", "children_are_mutually_exclusive": true}]. This enables mutual exclusivity on the existing Energy parent tag so only one child energy level can be assigned at a time.
Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-website-001", "flagged": true, "add_tags": ["High Priority"]}]. This flags the project and adds the tag in a single call.
Call reorder_task with task_id='task-qa-001', before_task_id='task-fe-001'. This moves QA testing before Frontend build.
Call delete_tags with tag_ids='tag-errands-001'. This permanently deletes the tag. Tasks that had this tag lose the association but are not deleted themselves.
Call get_perspectives (no parameters). The response includes name, type (built-in or custom), and id. Filter the results for type='custom' to see only custom perspectives.
Call create_folders with [{"name": "Q2", "parent_path": "Work"}]. This creates the Q2 folder nested inside the Work folder.
Call switch_perspective with perspective_name='Weekly Review'.

Two steps:
1. Call get_tasks with overdue=true to see all overdue tasks.
2. After reviewing results and identifying the 3 most urgent, call update_tasks with [{"id": "<id1>", "flagged": true}, {"id": "<id2>", "flagged": true}, {"id": "<id3>", "flagged": true}] to flag them.

Call get_projects with query='Quantum Computing Research' to check if the project exists. If found, call get_tasks with project_id set to the returned project ID. If not found, inform the user the project doesn't exist. Alternatively, call get_tasks with query='Quantum Computing Research' to search for tasks matching that name across all projects.
Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Buy birthday gift", "tags": ["Computer", "Errands"]}]. No project_id or parent_task_id means it goes to the inbox. Tags are passed as a list of strings.

Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Buy groceries"}, {"task_name": "Do laundry"}, {"task_name": "Wash dishes"}, {"task_name": "Vacuum living room"}, {"task_name": "Prep meals for the week"}]. No project_id means they go to the inbox.
Call create_projects with [{"name": "Kitchen Renovation", "project_type": "sequential", "folder_path": "Home"}, {"name": "Garden Planning", "project_type": "parallel", "folder_path": "Home"}].

Two steps:
1. Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Event Planning", "project_id": "proj-100", "sequential": true}] to create the parent action group.
2. Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Research venues", "parent_task_id": "<event-planning-task-id>"}, {"task_name": "Book venue", "parent_task_id": "<event-planning-task-id>"}, {"task_name": "Send invitations", "parent_task_id": "<event-planning-task-id>"}] to add the subtasks. The sequential flag on the parent ensures they must be done in order.

Multi-step approach using action groups:
1. Call create_projects with [{"name": "Home Renovation", "project_type": "parallel"}] to create a parallel project.
2. Create two sequential action groups as tasks in the project:
   - create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Contractor quotes chain", "project_id": "<project-id>", "sequential": true}, {"task_name": "Paint selection chain", "project_id": "<project-id>", "sequential": true}]
3. Add subtasks to each chain:
   - create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Get contractor quotes", "parent_task_id": "<contractor-chain-id>"}, {"task_name": "Schedule contractor", "parent_task_id": "<contractor-chain-id>"}]
   - create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Choose paint colors", "parent_task_id": "<paint-chain-id>"}, {"task_name": "Buy paint", "parent_task_id": "<paint-chain-id>"}]
Because the project is parallel, both chains are available simultaneously. Within each sequential chain, tasks proceed in order.

Three steps:
1. create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Prepare presentation", "project_id": "proj-200", "sequential": true}] — creates the parent action group with sequential subtask ordering.
2. create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Research topic", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>"}, {"task_name": "Write outline", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>"}, {"task_name": "Create slides", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>", "sequential": false}] — adds sequential subtasks; 'Create slides' is itself a parallel action group (sequential=false, though this is actually set on the task as a parent property).

Actually, correction: sequential is set on the parent to control how its children are ordered. So:
1. create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Prepare presentation", "project_id": "proj-200", "sequential": true}]
2. create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Research topic", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>"}, {"task_name": "Write outline", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>"}, {"task_name": "Create slides", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>"}]
3. Then add parallel subtasks under 'Create slides': create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Design template", "parent_task_id": "<create-slides-id>"}, {"task_name": "Add content", "parent_task_id": "<create-slides-id>"}, {"task_name": "Add animations", "parent_task_id": "<create-slides-id>"}]. Since 'Create slides' defaults to parallel (sequential not set or false), these subtasks will all be available simultaneously.

OmniFocus doesn't support true diamond/converging dependencies natively. The closest approximation:
1. Create a sequential action group in proj-300: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Report workflow", "project_id": "proj-300", "sequential": true}]
2. Create a parallel sub-group for the two independent tasks: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Independent work", "parent_task_id": "<workflow-id>", "sequential": false}]
3. Add the independent tasks: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Draft report", "parent_task_id": "<independent-id>"}, {"task_name": "Collect data", "parent_task_id": "<independent-id>"}]
4. Add the dependent task after the parallel group in the sequential parent: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Review findings", "parent_task_id": "<workflow-id>"}]

This works because the sequential parent ('Report workflow') won't make 'Review findings' available until the parallel sub-group ('Independent work') is completed (i.e., both 'Draft report' and 'Collect data' are done, assuming completed_by_children is true on the parallel group). You may need to set completed_by_children=true on the 'Independent work' task.

Call update_tasks with [{"id": "task-weekly-123", "recurrence": "", "status": "dropped"}]. Per the server instructions: to stop a recurring series, clear the recurrence first and drop in the same call. This prevents the drop from spawning a new occurrence.
Call update_tags with [{"id": "tag-aimee-001", "parent_tag": "People"}]. This moves the tag under the 'People' parent. Task associations are preserved — only the tag hierarchy changes.
